name: Bird ID: Western North America
description: Identify 55 signature birds of the American West from photos, from coastal chaparral to desert and mountain pine forest. Details give the scientific name, key field marks, and lookalike tips.

fields.extra: Cyanocitta stelleri. Deep blue jay with a sooty-black head and tall shaggy crest; the West's only crested jay. Bold camp-robber of conifer forests.
Aphelocoma californica. Crestless azure-blue jay with a gray-brown back, whitish throat, and partial blue necklace. Steller's jay, by contrast, is darker with a crest.
Callipepla californica. Plump gray-brown quail with a comma-shaped head plume and a scaled belly with a chestnut patch. Gambel's quail is its desert twin with an unscaled cream belly.
Calypte anna. Male's entire crown and gorget flash iridescent rose-pink; body green and gray. Year-round resident of the Pacific coast; males make a loud chirp at the bottom of display dives.
Pica hudsonia. Unmistakable pied corvid: black-and-white body, iridescent blue-green wings, and a very long tail. Yellow-billed magpie of California's Central Valley differs only in bill color.
Pipilo maculatus. Black hood and back with white-spotted wings, rufous flanks, red eye. The West's version of the eastern towhee; scratches loudly in leaf litter.
Melozone crissalis. Plain matte-brown, sparrow-shaped yard bird with rusty undertail coverts and a buffy throat. Hops around patios; gives a sharp metallic 'chink'.
Sialia mexicana. Male deep blue with a rusty breast that wraps up onto the shoulders and back; blue (not rusty) throat. Mountain bluebird lacks any rust at all.
Sialia currucoides. Male sky-blue all over with no rust anywhere; female gray with blue wing and tail tinges. Hovers over open meadows before dropping on insects.
Piranga ludoviciana. Breeding male has a flame-red head on a yellow body with black back, wings, and tail. Conifer-forest breeder that shows up in yards on migration.
Pheucticus melanocephalus. Male burnt-orange with a black head and black-and-white wings, plus the massive grosbeak bill. The West's counterpart of the rose-breasted grosbeak; rich robin-like song.
Passerina amoena. Male turquoise-blue above with a cinnamon breast band and white wing bars and belly. The indigo bunting of the East is solid blue with no rust or wing bars.

Icterus bullockii. Male orange with a black crown, black eye-line, small black bib, and big white wing patch; face mostly orange. Hooded oriole male instead has an orange crown and larger black face-and-bib patch.
Icterus cucullatus. Slim oriole; male has an orange-yellow hood over the crown with a black face and throat bib, and a longer, slightly curved bill. Loves palms - nests sewn under palm fronds.

Spinus psaltria. Tiny goldfinch; male has a black cap with greenish or black back and yellow underparts, plus white wing flashes. American goldfinch males are brighter lemon with a yellow (not dark) back.
Spinus pinus. Heavily streaked little finch with a sharp thin bill and yellow flashes in the wings and tail. Roams in flocks with goldfinches; numbers irrupt some winters.
Poecile gambeli. The only chickadee with a white eyebrow stripe cutting through its black cap. Dry mountain conifer forests; hoarse 'chick-a-dee' call.
Poecile rufescens. Richly colored chickadee with a chestnut back and (in most of its range) chestnut flanks. Humid Pacific coast forests from California to Alaska.
Baeolophus inornatus. Plain gray-brown crested bird of California oak woodland - no other marks at all. Its drabness plus the crest IS the field mark.
Psaltriparus minimus. Tiny plain gray ball of a bird with a long tail and stubby bill; almost always in busy chattering flocks that stream from bush to bush. Weaves a hanging sock nest.
Sitta pygmaea. Very small nuthatch with a gray-brown cap down to the eye and buffy underparts; swarms ponderosa pines in peeping flocks and roosts communally in cavities.
Thryomanes bewickii. Brown-gray wren with a long white eyebrow and a long tail edged in white that it flicks sideways. Common in western brush and yards.

Campylorhynchus brunneicapillus. Huge spotted wren of the desert Southwest with a bold white eyebrow and barred wings and tail. Builds football-shaped nests in cholla; song like a car that won't start.
Catherpes mexicanus. Rusty wren with a contrasting white throat and breast, long slightly curved bill. Its sweet cascading song tumbles down canyon walls.

Toxostoma curvirostre. Gray-brown desert thrasher with a sickle-shaped bill, orange eye, and blurry breast spots. Gives a sharp liquid 'whit-wheet!' from cholla. California thrasher is darker, plain-breasted, with a dark eye.

Toxostoma redivivum. Large dusky-brown chaparral skulker with a deeply curved bill, plain unspotted breast, and dark eye. Curve-billed thrasher (deserts) is paler with an orange eye and spotted breast.
Phainopepla nitens. Silky flycatcher of desert mistletoe: male glossy black with a wispy crest, red eye, and white wing patches visible in flight; female gray.
Auriparus flaviceps. Tiny gray desert bird with a lemon-yellow head and a chestnut shoulder patch. Builds conspicuous spherical stick nests in thorny scrub.
Chamaea fasciata. Plain gray-brown chaparral bird with a pale eye and a long, often-cocked tail; far more often heard than seen. Song is an accelerating bouncing-ball trill.

Melanerpes formicivorus. Clown-faced woodpecker: red cap, white forehead and cheek, staring pale eye. Lives in family groups that drill 'granary' trees with thousands of acorn holes; laughing 'waka-waka' call.
Melanerpes uropygialis. Zebra-backed woodpecker of saguaro desert with a plain tan head and belly; male has a small round red cap. Nests in holes in giant cacti.
Melanerpes lewis. Odd dark woodpecker with a glossy green-black back, gray collar, dark red face, and pinkish belly; flies with slow, crow-like flaps, catching insects in midair.
Dryobates albolarvatus. The only North American woodpecker with a black body and an all-white head; male adds a red nape spot. Pine forests of the far-western mountains.
Colaptes auratus. Western 'red-shafted' birds flash salmon-red wing and tail linings (yellow in the East) and males have a red mustache stripe. Barred brown back, black chest crescent, white rump.
Geococcyx californianus. Lanky streaked ground cuckoo with a shaggy crest, long tail, and strong legs; sprints after lizards and snakes and rarely flies.
Callipepla gambelii. Desert quail with a comma-shaped black plume; male has a black face and belly patch on a plain cream belly. California quail has a scaled belly instead.
Sturnella neglecta. Chunky grassland bird with a bright yellow breast crossed by a black V, streaky brown back, and white outer tail feathers. Rich flute-like song from fence posts.
Euphagus cyanocephalus. Male glossy black with purple-green sheen and a staring yellow eye; female plain brown with a dark eye. Struts on parking lots and lawns.
Quiscalus mexicanus. Male huge, glossy violet-black with a yellow eye and an extravagant keel-shaped tail; female smaller and warm brown. Raucous colonies in city trees across the Southwest.
Xanthocephalus xanthocephalus. Male black with a brilliant yellow head and breast and white wing patches. Nests in prairie marshes; song like a rusty gate.
Zonotrichia leucophrys. Clean gray breast under a boldly black-and-white striped crown; pink-orange bill. Golden-crowned sparrow instead has a yellow central crown stripe.
Zonotrichia atricapilla. Winter yards on the Pacific coast: dusky sparrow with a yellow forecrown bordered by black. Sings three descending whistles, 'oh-dear-me'.
Sayornis nigricans. Dapper sooty-black flycatcher with a crisp white belly; pumps its tail from low perches near water. Say's phoebe is gray-brown with a cinnamon belly.
Sayornis saya. Soft gray-brown flycatcher with a cinnamon-buff belly and black tail; wags its tail from open perches in dry country. Black phoebe is black with a white belly.
Tyrannus verticalis. Gray-headed flycatcher with a lemon-yellow belly and a black tail with white outer edges. Perches on fences and wires, fearlessly chasing off hawks.
Tachycineta thalassina. Emerald-and-violet back with white below; the white of the face wraps above the eye, and white flank patches nearly meet over the rump - both marks the tree swallow lacks.
Cinclus mexicanus. Chunky slate-gray songbird of rushing mountain streams; bobs constantly on wet rocks, then walks and swims underwater to hunt insect larvae.

Ixoreus naevius. Like a robin wearing extra trim: burnt-orange breast with a black (male) breast band, orange eyebrow and wing bars, blue-gray back. Eerie single-note whistle in Pacific Northwest forests.
Nucifraga columbiana. Gray corvid with black wings, flashing white wing patches and tail edges, and a long dagger bill. Caches tens of thousands of pine seeds and remembers where.
Pica nuttalli. Found nowhere on Earth but California's Central Valley and coastal ranges: a magpie with a yellow bill and yellow skin around the eye. Otherwise like the black-billed magpie.

Corvus corax. Massive all-black corvid with a heavy Roman-nosed bill, shaggy throat feathers, and a wedge-shaped tail in flight; deep croaking calls. Crows are smaller with fan-shaped tails and 'caw' calls.
Selasphorus rufus. Male burnt-orange nearly all over with a scarlet gorget; ferociously territorial at feeders. Breeds far north to Alaska, migrating through the Rockies each fall.
Archilochus alexandri. Male's gorget looks plain black until a violet band flashes at its lower edge; green above, dingy below. The widespread summer hummingbird of the interior West.
Athene cunicularia. Small long-legged owl that stands on the ground by day at burrow entrances, bobbing when agitated; sandy-spotted with fierce yellow eyes.

Gymnogyps californianus. North America's largest flying bird (nearly 3 m wingspan): black with bold white underwing triangles and a bare orange-pink head. Rescued from a wild population of zero in 1987 by captive breeding.
